MEMO TO FILE 
December 2, 1975 
RE: 
Telephone inquiry from Don Reisner, 
registered lobbyist for the 
California Railroad Association 
Y/7/ 
He wanted to know what restrictions and reporting requirements 
apply to the following situation: 
He and his wife plan to send approximately twenty 
Christmas cards to state legislators, legislative 
officials and agency officials. 
He would pay for the 
cards from his own funds except that Section 86106 
requires payments "in connection with'' lobbying activities 
to be made from the lobbyist account, and then provides 
that " [a]ny lobbyist who makes a gift to an elected 
state official, a legislative official or an agency 
official is deemed to be acting in connection with his 
activities as a lobbyist." 
I told him to send the cards, pay for them in the usual fashion 
and not report the cost. 
Although a strict interpretation of 
statute and regulation might lead to another conclusion, this 
situation seems too trivial to come within the Act .... further­
more, any other advice would not be in keeping with the Holiday 
spirit. 
In my opinion, the second sentence of Section 86106 should be 
deleted by statutory amendment.

AIR QUALITY 
MICHAEL WORNUM 
ASSEMBL.YMAN, NINTH DISTRICT 
October 2, 1975 
Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. o. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Mr. Lowenstein: 
I am writing on behalf of a constituent who is currently 
serving as a trustee of a local school district. 
As the conflict of interest section of Proposition 9 is 
drafted, and adopted, certain individuals may be faced with 
the decision to betray their clients' rights to privacy or 
be forced to resign from public service. 
Mr. William Eckhoff, CPA, is one such person. 
Law does not 
allow him the same confidential relationship with clients 
that doctors and lawyers enjoy. 
He, therefore, may well 
decide to resign his seat at a great loss to the District 
he represents. 
I would like to encourage guidelines which would acknowledge 
such private client relationships, if at all legally possible. 
I would further appreciate an indication of whether or not 
this would be possible. 
Thank you. 
MW:dhc 
cc: 
Mr. Douglas Maloney 
Mr. William Eckhoff 
Sincerely, 
Michael Wornum

sections.question: Whether there is a requirement under the Political Reform Act for a certified public accountant serving as a school district trustee to file disclosure statements, and whether legally recognized privileges can be implicitly recognized in the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: There is no present requirement under the Political Reform Act that Mr. Eckhoff or any other school district trustee file disclosure statements until the school district has adopted a conflict of interest code. The Commission will consider whether legally recognized privileges are implicitly recognized in the disclosure provisions of the Act, and the Executive Director is not aware of any legal theory whereby privileges could be recognized beyond those established by law.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a CPA serving as a school district trustee need to file disclosure statements under the Political Reform Act, and can professional privileges be recognized in disclosure requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: School district trustees have no disclosure requirement until their district adopts a conflict of interest code. The FPPC will consider in the future whether professional privileges can be implicitly recognized in the Political Reform Act's disclosure provisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate items: (1) formal FPPC advice letter response dated October 6, 1975 from Michael Bennett to Assemblysman Wornum dated October 2, 1975 regarding school trustee disclosure requirements; (2) separate memo to file dated December 2, 1975 regarding unrelated telephone inquiry about Christmas card reporting requirements. Extraction focused on primary advice letter regarding the main substantive question.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether there is a requirement under the Political Reform Act for a certified public accountant serving as a school district trustee to file disclosure statements, and whether legally recognized privileges can be implicitly recognized in the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: There is no present requirement under the Political Reform Act that Mr. Eckhoff or any other school district trustee file disclosure statements until the school district has adopted a conflict of interest code. The Commission will consider whether legally recognized privileges are implicitly recognized in the disclosure provisions of the Act, and the Executive Director is not aware of any legal theory whereby privileges could be recognized beyond those established by law.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Assemblyman Wornum's inquiry about disclosure requirements for a CPA school trustee, clarifying that no disclosure filing is required until a conflict of interest code is adopted, while indicating future consideration of whether professional privileges can be protected under the Act.
